solutions.title: Tutorial
solutions.content:
1533I - Excursions
There is a linear solution to this problem (which I'll describe briefly at the end of this editorial), but our model solution is $$$O(n^4)$$$, that's why the constraints are that low. Guess I'm much less smarter than the top participants, I didn't see any linear solutions before the contest.
Okay, my solution uses minimum cost flows (though it can be formulated with matchings as well). Suppose that all tourists from sight $$$j$$$ in Engels go to sight $$$i$$$ in Saratov. It means that for every sight $$$v$$$ in Saratov such that $$$v \ne i$$$ and there's a bus route from $$$v$$$ to $$$j$$$, we can send all tourists from the sight $$$v$$$ to the sight $$$j$$$, and they won't return to the same sight where they started. Let's say that the sight $$$j$$$ in Engels "fixes" the sight $$$v$$$ in Saratov, if all tourists from sight $$$v$$$ go to sight $$$j$$$ and don't return to the sight $$$v$$$. Obviously, each sight $$$j$$$ in Engels can fix at most $$$d_j - 1$$$ sights in Saratov, where $$$d_j$$$ is the number of bus routes going to the sight $$$j$$$. The answer to the problem is the minimum possible total number of people in the sights of Saratov that are not "fixed".
Let's build a flow network as follows: each sight is represented by a vertex; for every sight $$$j$$$ in Engels, there is an arc from the source to the vertex representing it with a capacity of $$$d_j - 1$$$ and a cost of $$$0$$$, meaning that this sight can "fix" up to $$$d_j - 1$$$ sights in Saratov; for every sight $$$i$$$ in Saratov, there is an arc from the vertex representing it to the sink with a capacity of $$$1$$$ and a cost of $$$-k_i$$$ (meaning that if the $$$i$$$-th sight in Saratov is "fixed", the cost of the flow reduces by $$$k_i$$$); and for every bus route connecting sight $$$x_i$$$ in Saratov and sight $$$y_i$$$ in Engels, there is an arc from the vertex representing $$$y_i$$$ to the vertex representing $$$x_i$$$ with a capacity of $$$1$$$ and a cost of $$$0$$$, meaning that the sight $$$y_i$$$ can "fix" the sight $$$x_i$$$.
Finding the minimum cost flow in this network allows us to "fix" the sights with the maximum possible number of people, so the answer is $$$\sum_{i = 1}^{n} k_i + cost$$$, where $$$cost$$$ is the cost of the flow.
A brief description of linear solution: let's build a bipartite graph representing the sights and bus routes (sights are represented by vertices, bus routes — by edges) and solve the problem for each component of this graph separately. It can be shown that if some component contains a cycle, all tourists in this component can be directed so that nobody returns to the same vertex where they started; otherwise, if a component doesn't contain any cycles, it's sufficient to choose the vertex of the left part with minimum possible $$$k_i$$$ in this component, and the tourists from this vertex will be the only ones which return where they started (all other tourists can be directed so that they move towards this chosen vertex).
